content.problemStatement:
You are given a tree rooted at node 111 with NNN vertices. The iiiththth vertex initially has value Ai(1‚â§i‚â§N)A_i (1 \leq i \leq N)Ai‚Äã(1‚â§i‚â§N). You are also given QQQ queries.

In each query you are given a vertex VVV.  Let S={S1,S2,...Sx}S = \{ S_1 , S_2 , ... S_x \} S={S1‚Äã,S2‚Äã,...Sx‚Äã} denote the set of vertices such that SiS_iSi‚Äã is in the subtree of VVV, distance between SiS_iSi‚Äã and VVV is even and Si‚âÝVS_i \neq VSi‚ÄãÓÄÝ=V for all iii. For all SiS_iSi‚Äã , add AAASiS_iSi‚Äã to AVA_VAV‚Äã and change the value of AAASiS_iSi‚Äã to zero.

Find the values of all the vertices after all queries are performed.

**Note**-The distance between two vertices is defined as the number of edges
traversed on the shortest path from one vertex to the other.

Input:
The first line contains an integer TTT denoting the number of test cases.
The first line of each test case contain two integers NNN and QQQ.
The second line contains NNN space separated integers, A1,A2,...,AnA_1, A_2, ..., A_nA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,...,An‚Äã denoting the initial values of the vertices.
The next N‚àí1N-1N‚àí1 lines contain two integers uuu and vvv denoting an edge between uuuand vvv.
The next QQQ lines contain a single integer which is the query.
Output:
Print a single line containing NNN integers for each test case which is the final values of the vertices.
###Constraints:


1‚â§T‚â§101\leq T \leq 101‚â§T‚â§10
1‚â§N‚â§2000001 \leq N \leq 2000001‚â§N‚â§200000
1‚â§Q‚â§2000001 \leq Q \leq 2000001‚â§Q‚â§200000
0‚â§Ai‚â§1090 \leq A_i \leq 10^90‚â§Ai‚Äã‚â§109
The sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 200000200000200000.
The sum of QQQ over all test cases does not exceed 200000200000200000.
###Sample Input
1
4 3
6 2 7 3
1 2
2 3
3 4
3
2
1

###Sample Output
13 5 0 0

###Explanation

Node 333 has no child in its subtree which is at  an even distance so there is no change in the values. Values of nodes after 1st1st1st query: 6,2,7,36, 2, 7, 36,2,7,3.

Node 444 is at an even distance in the subtree of node 222 so A4A_4A4‚Äã gets added to A2A_2A2‚Äã and A4A_4A4‚Äã becomes 0. Values of nodes after 2nd2nd2nd query: 6,5,7,06, 5, 7, 06,5,7,0.

Node 333 is at an even distance in the subtree of node 111 so A3A_3A3‚Äã gets added to A1A_1A1‚Äã and A3A_3A3‚Äã becomes 0. Values of nodes after 3rd3rd3rd query: 13,5,0,013, 5, 0, 013,5,0,0.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases ‚â§ 200000
The sum of Q over all test cases ‚â§ 200000
content.inputFormat:
The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case:
- The first line contains two integers N and Q ‚Äì the number of vertices in the tree and the number of queries.
- The second line contains N integers A1 ‚Ä¶ AN ‚Äì the initial value of each vertex (1‚Äëindexed).
- The next N‚Äë1 lines each contain two integers u and v denoting an undirected edge of the tree. The tree is rooted at vertex 1.
- The next Q lines each contain a single integer V ‚Äì the vertex given in the i‚Äëth query.
All vertices are numbered from 1 to N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with N space‚Äëseparated integers ‚Äì the final values of vertices 1 ‚Ä¶ N after processing all queries.

solution.approach:
1. Root the tree at 1 and run a DFS to compute for every vertex:
   - depth (0 for the root),
   - tin[v] ‚Äì the time when the vertex is first visited,
   - tout[v] ‚Äì the last time inside its subtree.
   The vertices of a subtree form a contiguous segment [tin, tout] in the Euler order.
2. Because only vertices whose distance to the queried vertex V is even are affected, the condition is equivalent to "depth parity of the vertex equals depth parity of V". Therefore we keep two independent segment trees:
   - one for all even‚Äëdepth vertices,
   - one for all odd‚Äëdepth vertices.
   In each tree the value at position tin[v] is the current value of v if its depth parity matches the tree, otherwise 0.
3. Each segment tree supports:
   - range sum query,
   - range assignment to 0 (lazy propagation),
   - point assignment (used to write back the new value of V).
   All operations work in O(log N).
4. Processing a query V:
   * let p = depth[V] % 2 and choose the corresponding segment tree.
   * l = tin[V], r = tout[V].
   * total = querySum(l, r) ‚Äì the sum of values of all vertices in V's subtree having the same parity.
   * curV = querySum(l, l) ‚Äì current value of V itself.
   * add = total - curV ‚Äì the amount that must be moved to V.
   * newV = curV + add.
   * assignZero(l, r) ‚Äì set the whole segment to 0.
   * assignPoint(l, newV) ‚Äì restore the updated value of V.
5. After all queries, the final value of each vertex v is obtained by a point query on the segment tree that matches its depth parity.
The whole algorithm runs in O((N + Q) log N) time and O(N) memory.
solution.timeComplexity: O((N + Q) ¬∑ log N) per test case
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) ‚Äì adjacency list, Euler arrays and two segment trees
